**Cinemax** is an American [premium television](/wiki/Premium_television "Premium television") network and service owned by [Home Box Office, Inc.](/wiki/Home_Box_Office,_Inc. "Home Box Office, Inc."), a subsidiary of [Warner Bros. Discovery](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Discovery "Warner Bros. Discovery"). Launched on August 1, 1980, as a "maxi-pay" service to complement the offerings of its sister premium network, [HBO](/wiki/HBO "HBO") (Home Box Office), Cinemax initially focused on recent and classic films. Today, its programming primarily includes recent and classic theatrically released films, original series, documentaries, and special behind-the-scenes features.

It operates five 24-hour linear channels and offers a traditional subscription video-on-demand service called Cinemax On Demand. Cinemax previously provided a TV Everywhere streaming platform, Cinemax Go, which is no longer available. However, its linear channels are accessible through multichannel video programming distributors such as Apple TV, Amazon Video, and Roku.[[1]](#cite_note-3)[[2]](#cite_note-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cinemax&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Home Box Office, Inc., owned by [Time Inc.](/wiki/Time_Inc. "Time Inc.")'s [Time-Life Broadcasting](/wiki/Time_Life "Time Life") unit, launched a movie-centered pay service called [Take 2](/wiki/Take_2_(TV_channel) "Take 2 (TV channel)") on April 1, 1979, as a companion to HBO. However, Take 2 struggled with subscriber growth and was discontinued in February 1981.[[3]](#cite_note-Movie_Duels-5) HBO then announced the launch of Cinemax, a "maxi-pay" network designed to complement HBO and compete with other movie network/premium services.[[4]](#cite_note-6) Cinemax debuted on August 1, 1980, focusing on theatrical films with no commercials or edits for content.[[5]](#cite_note-7)

Cinemax initially aired on 56 cable systems in the [Eastern](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone "Eastern Time Zone") and [Central](/wiki/Central_Time_Zone "Central Time Zone") time zones, with a West Coast feed launching on September 1, 1980.[[6]](#cite_note-8)[[7]](#cite_note-9) The network transitioned to a 24-hour schedule on January 1, 1981.[[8]](#cite_note-b&c-max24hours-10)[[9]](#cite_note-11)[[10]](#cite_note-12) Cinemax offered uncut films and was marketed as a premium addition to HBO, often bundled with HBO for a discount.

In October 1983, Cinemax faced a trademark infringement lawsuit from [Tulsa 23 Limited Partnership](/wiki/KOKI-TV "KOKI-TV") over its promotional slogan, "We Are Your Movie Star."[[11]](#cite_note-13) The court ruled in favor of Tulsa 23, and Cinemax was ordered to discontinue the slogan.[[12]](#cite_note-14)[[13]](#cite_note-15)

As competition increased, Cinemax adjusted its programming strategy by including more adult-oriented films and launching the *Friday After Dark* late-night block in 1984. The network also introduced original programming and series, including *[Second City Television](/wiki/Second_City_Television "Second City Television"),* *[The Richard Belzer Show](/wiki/Richard_Belzer "Richard Belzer")* and *[The Max Headroom Show](/wiki/The_Max_Headroom_Show "The Max Headroom Show")*, as well as the freeform *[Cinemax Comedy Experiment](/wiki/Cinemax_Comedy_Experiment "Cinemax Comedy Experiment")*. Music programming was also introduced, including *Album Flash* and *Cinemax Sessions*; some of these music programs were simulcast for free over a national FM radio network to provide stereo audio.[[14]](#cite_note-16) In February 1988, the network premiere of *[Lethal Weapon](/wiki/Lethal_Weapon "Lethal Weapon")* became one of Cinemax's highest-rated broadcasts.[[15]](#cite_note-17)

On March 4, 1989, Warner Communications announced its intent to merge with HBO's parent company, Time Inc., for $14.9 billion. Despite legal challenges from [Paramount Communications](/wiki/Gulf_and_Western_Industries "Gulf and Western Industries"), the merger was completed on January 10, 1990, creating Time Warner (later WarnerMedia, now Warner Bros. Discovery), which remains the parent company of Cinemax and HBO.[[16]](#cite_note-18)[[17]](#cite_note-19)

In August 1991, Cinemax, along with HBO, was among the first American pay TV services to introduce multiplexed channels. Starting in 1992, Cinemax reintroduced television series development with adult-oriented scripted series, such as *Erotic Confessions*, *Hot Line*, *[Passion Cove](/wiki/Passion_Cove "Passion Cove")*, [*Lingerie*](/wiki/Lingerie_(TV_series) "Lingerie (TV series)"), and *[Co-Ed Confidential](/wiki/Co-Ed_Confidential "Co-Ed Confidential")*.

Cinemax was the exclusive premiere network of several blockbuster movies, such as *[GoodFellas](/wiki/GoodFellas "GoodFellas")* and *[The Godfather Part III](/wiki/The_Godfather_Part_III "The Godfather Part III")* in 1991, *[Juice](/wiki/Juice_(1992_film) "Juice (1992 film)")* in 1993, *[The Fugitive](/wiki/The_Fugitive_(1993_film) "The Fugitive (1993 film)")* in 1994, *[Any Given Sunday](/wiki/Any_Given_Sunday "Any Given Sunday")*, *[Eyes Wide Shut](/wiki/Eyes_Wide_Shut "Eyes Wide Shut")*, and *[The Matrix](/wiki/The_Matrix "The Matrix")* in 2000, and *[X-Men](/wiki/X-Men_(2000_film) "X-Men (2000 film)")* in 2002. From 1992 to 1997, Cinemax featured daily movie showcases centered on specific genres, identified by pictograms.[[18]](#cite_note-Cinemax_makeover-20)[[19]](#cite_note-21) This practice ended in September 1997, with Cinemax adopting a simplified branding strategy that included "Max Hits at 8" and "Max Prime at 10."[[20]](#cite_note-MaxMovieFan-22)

In February 1994, Cinemax, HBO, Showtime, and The Movie Channel introduced a [content advisory system](/wiki/United_States_pay_television_content_advisory_system "United States pay television content advisory system") to inform viewers about program content.[[21]](#cite_note-23) A revised system with content codes was implemented on June 10, 1994.[[22]](#cite_note-24)

In 1998, Cinemax began offering "sneak preview" blocks for its multiplex channels, ActionMax and ThrillerMax. Classic films, once a staple of the Cinemax schedule, were increasingly featured on multiplex channels like 5StarMax.

In February 2011, Cinemax shifted its focus to original programming, launching series to compete with other premium services, all of which were very popular: *[Banshee](/wiki/Banshee_(TV_series) "Banshee (TV series)"),* *[The Knick](/wiki/The_Knick "The Knick")*, *[Hunted](/wiki/Hunted_(2012_TV_series) "Hunted (2012 TV series)")*, *[Strike Back](/wiki/Strike_Back_(TV_series) "Strike Back (TV series)")*. and to rebrand its image from being primarily known for softcore pornographic content.[[23]](#cite_note-:2-25) Cinemax shortened its name to just Max in 2008, but it was reverted 3 years later.

Following [AT&T](/wiki/AT%26T "AT&T")'s acquisition of Time Warner in 2018, Cinemax's focus shifted significantly. Adult programming on Cinemax and HBO's multiplexes, as well as on-demand services, nearly disappeared due to increased availability of such content online and competition from other providers.[[23]](#cite_note-:2-25)

In January 2020, WarnerMedia announced that with the upcoming launch of [HBO Max](/wiki/HBO_Max "HBO Max"), Cinemax would cease commissioning original programming but would continue as a movie-focused service through existing distributors. Cinemax's original programming, although available internationally, was not included in HBO Max.[[24]](#cite_note-:0-26) Len Amato, the executive overseeing Cinemax and HBO's film and miniseries division, left WarnerMedia on August 14, 2020, marking the end of Cinemax's original programming efforts.[[25]](#cite_note-27)

On May 17, 2021, AT&T and [Discovery, Inc.](/wiki/Discovery,_Inc. "Discovery, Inc.") announced a [Reverse Morris Trust](/wiki/Reverse_Morris_Trust "Reverse Morris Trust") agreement, wherein WarnerMedia would [spin-off](/wiki/Corporate_spin-off "Corporate spin-off") from AT&T and merge with Discovery for $43 billion. This merger, completed in spring 2022, resulted in the formation of Warner Bros. Discovery, led by [David Zaslav](/wiki/David_Zaslav "David Zaslav").[[26]](#cite_note-28)[[27]](#cite_note-29)[[28]](#cite_note-30)[[29]](#cite_note-31) On May 23, 2023, the merged company's streaming service was rebranded as Max. It would soon revert back to HBO Max on July 9, 2025.

## Channels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cinemax&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Channels")]

On May 8, 1991, Home Box Office Inc. announced plans to launch multiplexed companion channels for HBO and Cinemax, offering additional programming options at no extra charge. The test launch on August 1, 1991, introduced Cinemax 2, HBO2, and HBO3 (now HBO Signature) to select TeleCable customers, providing distinct programming from their parent channels.[[30]](#cite_note-32)[[31]](#cite_note-33)[[32]](#cite_note-34)[[33]](#cite_note-35)[[34]](#cite_note-36)[[35]](#cite_note-37) John K. Billock, HBO's EVP of marketing, cited subscriber dissatisfaction with program scheduling as a reason for the multiplex expansion. A November 1991 [ACNielsen](/wiki/ACNielsen "ACNielsen") survey showed positive impacts from the multiplex channels, including reduced negative opinions on pricing and improved overall usage.[[36]](#cite_note-38)[[37]](#cite_note-39)

In February 1996, Home Box Office announced plans to expand to twelve channels, including two additional Cinemax channels and a fourth HBO channel, projected for a spring 1997 launch.[[38]](#cite_note-40) The Cinemax multiplex expanded on December 1, 1996, with the launch of Cinemax 3, and the introduction of [Mountain Time Zone](/wiki/Mountain_Time_Zone "Mountain Time Zone") feeds.[[39]](#cite_note-41)

The Cinemax multiplex was marketed as "MultiChannel Cinemax" in September 1994, rebranded to "MultiMax" in April 1998, and later applied to the expanded four-channel package.[[40]](#cite_note-42) This included MoreMax (formerly Cinemax 2), ActionMax (formerly Cinemax 3), and the new ThrillerMax channel.[[41]](#cite_note-HBO_digital_networks-43)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Cinemax_logo_%281997_-_2011%29.png/250px-Cinemax_logo_%281997_-_2011%29.png)](/wiki/File:Cinemax_logo_(1997_-_2011).png)

Cinemax logo used from 1997 to 2011. "Cine" was removed from the logo in 2008.

On May 17, 2001, four additional themed channels were launched: OuterMax, WMax (later MovieMax), @Max (later Max Latino), and 5StarMax.[[42]](#cite_note-Plex-44)[[43]](#cite_note-Four_Plexes-45) Max Latino was rebranded as Cinemáx on April 1, 2015, mirroring the flagship Cinemax schedule with Spanish-language dubs. MovieMax started as a family-oriented channel and later focused on recent and classic movies.[[44]](#cite_note-46)[[45]](#cite_note-47)

### List of channels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cinemax&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: List of channels")]

"ActionMax" redirects here. For the 1980s video game system, see [Action Max](/wiki/Action_Max "Action Max").

For the UK channel formerly known as ActionMax, see [Movies4Men 2](/wiki/Movies4Men_2 "Movies4Men 2").

"MovieMax" redirects here. For the Canadian premium movie service formerly known as MovieMax!, see [Encore Avenue](/wiki/Encore_Avenue "Encore Avenue").

"5StarMax" redirects here; not to be confused with [5Star](/wiki/5Star "5Star").

Cinemax offers up to five 24-hour multiplex channels, available in both [standard](/wiki/Standard-definition_television "Standard-definition television") and [high definition](/wiki/High-definition_television "High-definition television"), with regional feeds based on time zones.[[46]](#cite_note-48) These channels include a [subscription video-on-demand](/wiki/Video_on_demand "Video on demand") service, Cinemax on Demand. Maintenance periods, ranging from 30 minutes to two hours, occur monthly during early morning hours before the 6:00 a.m. ET/PT start of the broadcast day.

Cinemax, MoreMax and ActionMax transmit both Eastern and Pacific Time Zone schedules. The time zone-based feeds are usually packaged together, resulting in a maximum three-hour difference in local airtimes between geographic locations. The opposite-region feed serves as a [timeshift channel](/wiki/Timeshift_channel "Timeshift channel"), allowing viewers to watch programs up to three hours later or four hours earlier than their local airtime. While most providers offer only the East and West Coast feeds of the main Cinemax channel, timeshifted feeds for other multiplex channels are available on platforms like DirecTV, [YouTube TV](/wiki/YouTube_TV "YouTube TV"), and [Hulu + Live TV](/wiki/Hulu_%2B_Live_TV "Hulu + Live TV").

### Other services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cinemax&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Other services")]

#### Cinemax HD

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cinemax&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Cinemax HD")]

**Cinemax HD** broadcasts in [1080i](/wiki/1080i "1080i") resolution with [Dolby Digital](/wiki/Dolby_Digital "Dolby Digital") 5.1 sound. Cinemax provides high definition simulcast feeds for its main channel and all four multiplex channels. Initially, from the 2008 expansion of HD simulcasts for the multiplex feeds, most pay television providers only offered the main Cinemax channel in HD, with HD availability for multiplex channels varying by market. As of 2025, most providers offer all five Cinemax multiplex channels in HD, either on a dedicated HD channel tier or as hybrid SD/HD feeds. The flagship network switched to exclusive high definition programming on September 1, 2008.[[50]](#cite_note-53)

#### Cinemax On Demand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cinemax&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Cinemax On Demand")]

**Cinemax On Demand** is the network's companion subscription video-on-demand (SVOD) service, available at no extra charge to subscribers of the linear television service. Launched in 2002,[[51]](#cite_note-54) it offers VOD content through select virtual MVPD services such as [DirecTV Stream](/wiki/DirecTV_Stream "DirecTV Stream") and Hulu, as well as on Cinemax's dedicated OTT video channels via Apple TV Channels, Amazon Video Channels, and [The Roku Channel](/wiki/Roku#The_Roku_Channel "Roku").[[52]](#cite_note-55)

#### Cinemax Go

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cinemax&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Cinemax Go")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/MaxGO.svg/250px-MaxGO.svg.png)](/wiki/File:MaxGO.svg)

**Cinemax Go** (formerly **MAX Go**) was a [TV Everywhere](/wiki/TV_Everywhere "TV Everywhere") [streaming service](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media") for Cinemax subscribers, available as a desktop website and through apps for Apple and Google Play devices, as well as most digital media players and game consoles. Launched on September 13, 2010, it mirrored the content of Cinemax On Demand,[[53]](#cite_note-56)[[54]](#cite_note-57) excluding live simulcasts of the linear channels. New episodes of the network's original programming were available at the same time as their wireline release.

The service was available to subscribers of AT&T U-verse,[[55]](#cite_note-58) Cox Communications,[[56]](#cite_note-59) DirecTV,[[57]](#cite_note-60) [Dish Network](/wiki/Dish_Network "Dish Network"),[[58]](#cite_note-61) [Suddenlink Communications](/wiki/Suddenlink_Communications "Suddenlink Communications"),[[59]](#cite_note-62) and [Charter Communications](/wiki/Charter_Communications "Charter Communications").[[60]](#cite_note-63) The MAX Go app was discontinued on April 30, 2020, and the desktop website, renamed Cinemax Go, continued until July 31, 2022.[[61]](#cite_note-64) The service was discontinued as Warner Bros. Discovery consolidated its streaming efforts under HBO Max, rebranded as Max on May 23, 2023, before reverting to HBO Max on July 9, 2025.[[62]](#cite_note-65)[[63]](#cite_note-66)[[47]](#cite_note-rebrand-49)

## Programming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cinemax&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Programming")]

Cinemax schedules movies for 20–24 hours daily on its main channel and 24 hours on its multiplex channels. Since June 2000, new theatrical and original movies have premiered every Sunday at 8:00 p.m. ET/PT. First-run films typically debut 10 months to a year after their theatrical release and within six months of DVD or VOD release.[[64]](#cite_note-67)[[65]](#cite_note-68)[[66]](#cite_note-69)

### Original programming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cinemax&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Original programming")]

Main article: [List of Cinemax original programming](/wiki/List_of_Cinemax_original_programming "List of Cinemax original programming")

On August 12, 2011, [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Cinemax expanded into original programming with the American premiere of the British action series [*Strike Back*](/wiki/Strike_Back_(TV_series) "Strike Back (TV series)"). The series, which had originally aired in the UK, was produced in partnership with HBO [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. Cinemax's second original series, [*Hunted*](/wiki/Hunted_(2012_TV_series) "Hunted (2012 TV series)"), premiered on October 19, 2012, in collaboration with [BBC One](/wiki/BBC_One "BBC One").[[67]](#cite_note-tvwise2-70) This was followed by [*Banshee*](/wiki/Banshee_(TV_series) "Banshee (TV series)") in 2013,[[68]](#cite_note-71) and in 2014, *Sandbox* and *[The Knick](/wiki/The_Knick "The Knick")*.[[69]](#cite_note-72)[[70]](#cite_note-73)

In 2020, Cinemax announced it would stop commissioning original programming to focus on HBO Max.[[24]](#cite_note-:0-26) The martial arts drama *Warrior* was the last original series on Cinemax, with its third season moving to HBO Max in April 2021.[[71]](#cite_note-:1-74)[[72]](#cite_note-75)

#### Max After Dark

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cinemax&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Max After Dark")]

Cinemax's late-night block, **Max After Dark**, featured softcore pornographic films and original series with TV-MA or R ratings, primarily for strong sexual content and nudity. This block, which began on May 4, 1984, as "Cinemax Friday After Dark", expanded to seven nights a week by the late 1990s.[[18]](#cite_note-Cinemax_makeover-20) Programs included *[Lingerie](/wiki/Lingerie_(TV_series) "Lingerie (TV series)")*, *[Life on Top](/wiki/Life_on_Top "Life on Top")*, [*Femme Fatales*](/wiki/Femme_Fatales_(TV_series) "Femme Fatales (TV series)"), and *Topless Prophet*.[[73]](#cite_note-76) The block was known for its lenient scheduling around mainstream films and series and was often humorously referred to as "Skinemax".[[74]](#cite_note-skinemax-77)

The adult content was not limited to the main Cinemax channel but also aired on multiplex channels like MoreMax, ActionMax, ThrillerMax, and OuterMax. However, channels like MovieMax and 5StarMax avoided adult programming. The block's presence, alongside other HBO channels, was partly due to the FCC's content regulations not applying to cable networks.

By 2013, Cinemax began reducing Max After Dark content to focus more on original programming, as internet porn's prevalence made the block's content seem relatively tame compared to HBO's original series.[[75]](#cite_note-Stealth-78)[[76]](#cite_note-79)[[77]](#cite_note-80)[[78]](#cite_note-81)[[79]](#cite_note-82)[[80]](#cite_note-83)

## International distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cinemax&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: International distribution")]

### Latin America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cinemax&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Latin America")]

A Latin American version of Cinemax was launched on September 5, 1993, airing movies old and new, as well as television series and miniseries. Starting June 1, 2010, it became an ad-supported basic cable channel in the region. Original series would air on [HBO's premium suite](/wiki/HBO_Latin_America_Group#Operating_channels "HBO Latin America Group") in [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America").

### Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cinemax&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Asia")]

Main article: [Cinemax (Asian TV channel)](/wiki/Cinemax_(Asian_TV_channel) "Cinemax (Asian TV channel)")

Cinemax began broadcasting in 1996, featuring action, comedy, science fiction, and western movies. The channel was known as **Max** from 2009 to 2012.

### Central Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cinemax&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Central Europe")]

Launched in February 2005, Cinemax focused on festival, indie, European, and classic films. **Cinemax** and **Cinemax 2** are available in Bulgaria, the Czech Republic, Hungary, Poland, Romania, and Slovakia, and expanded to certain [Balkan](/wiki/Balkan_peninsula "Balkan peninsula") countries in 2009. Initially a timeshift channel, **Cinemax 2** started airing a distinct schedule from 2016.
